skill.name: posthog-automation
skill.description: Automate PostHog analytics and feature flag workflows through Rube MCP. It helps inspect projects, capture events, manage flags, and verify user access.
skill.summary: Use Rube MCP tools to operate PostHog events, projects, feature flags, profiles, and connection checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static shell-execution and weak-cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and ordinary documentation text in SKILL.md. The confirmed risk is networked automation through Rube MCP that can read PostHog project data, capture events, inspect user profiles, and create active feature flags.

medium_findings.title: Networked PostHog Automation Can Modify Production Analytics State
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to connect to Rube MCP and use PostHog tools that capture events, create feature flags, list projects, and retrieve profile data. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can alter external PostHog state or expose organization data if used with broad credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly requires an external Rube MCP connection and names PostHog tools that read and modify remote resources. The actions match the stated purpose, so the issue is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many reported Ruby or shell backtick alerts correspond to Markdown inline code spans around Rube and PostHog tool names, parameters, or examples. No evidence of executable Ruby, shell commands, command substitution, or user-controlled command construction was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are visible in Markdown prose and tables, not in a script or executable source file. The repository contains only SKILL.md, so there is no surrounding code path that could execute them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Unsupported By Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts at the description and user-profile lines do not correspond to cryptographic calls or algorithms. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic logic in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive text about PostHog automation and user profile contents. They contain no cryptographic API usage or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Refer To PostHog Metadata Lookup
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to instructions for extracting PostHog organization, project, feature flag, and pagination identifiers. These are remote application identifiers needed for PostHog workflows, not host system discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is limited to PostHog API workflow guidance and does not mention local OS, network, process, or filesystem enumeration. It still involves remote metadata access, so least-privilege PostHog credentials are important.

dangerous_patterns.title: External MCP Endpoint And Remote Tool Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires connecting to Rube MCP and then invoking PostHog tools over that connection. This is not malicious by itself, but it expands trust to an external service and authenticated PostHog account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external endpoint and remote tool names are explicitly documented. There is no evidence of hidden exfiltration, but remote authenticated operations are a real security boundary.

content.value_statement: PostHog work often requires exact tool names, project IDs, and safe sequencing. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Rube MCP workflows for analytics and feature flags.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for Rube MCP and PostHog authentication.
Captures PostHog events with distinct IDs, properties, and timestamps.
Lists and filters project events with dates, people, limits, and offsets.
Lists, retrieves, and creates PostHog feature flags for a project.
Resolves organization, project, and feature flag identifiers before actions.
Checks current PostHog user profile and API access details.
limitations: Requires an active Rube MCP connection and PostHog authorization.
Depends on current Rube tool schemas, so tools must be searched first.
Can affect live analytics or feature flags if used with production credentials.
Does not define rollback workflows for event capture or feature flag changes.

use_cases.title: Audit Feature Flag Configuration
use_cases.description: Review existing PostHog feature flags, inspect targeting rules, and confirm rollout percentages before a launch.

use_cases.title: Investigate Analytics Events
use_cases.description: Filter PostHog events by project, event name, person, and date range to validate tracking behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare Integration Checks
use_cases.description: Verify Rube MCP access, resolve project identifiers, and send controlled test events during implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Check PostHog Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to verify my Rube MCP PostHog connection. Search available tools first, then confirm the current user and active connection status.

prompt_templates.title: Find Recent Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Search current PostHog tool schemas, resolve my project ID, then list recent signup events from the last seven days with pagination guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Review Feature Flag
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the feature flag named new-dashboard-beta, retrieve its details, and summarize targeting groups, rollout percentage, and activation status.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Safe Flag Creation
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a safe PostHog feature flag creation workflow for a staged rollout. Search schemas first and ask before making any live change.

output_examples.input: Verify whether my PostHog connection is ready.
output_examples.output: The assistant searches available Rube tools, checks connection status, calls the current user endpoint, and reports whether PostHog access is active.

output_examples.input: Review the rollout for the checkout redesign flag.
output_examples.output: The assistant resolves the project, locates the feature flag, retrieves details, and summarizes targeting rules, rollout percentage, and activation state.
The assistant resolves the project ID, applies event and date filters, paginates results, and summarizes counts and notable property patterns.

best_practices: Search Rube tools before each workflow to confirm current schemas.
Use least-privilege PostHog credentials, especially for production projects.
Ask for explicit approval before creating active feature flags or sending events.
anti_patterns: Do not run production feature flag changes without reviewing targeting rules.
Do not assume project names are unique; resolve numeric project IDs first.
Do not capture events with inconsistent names or missing distinct IDs.

faq.question: Does this skill require PostHog credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires an active PostHog connection through Rube MCP before it can run workflows.

faq.question: Can it create feature flags?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents PostHog feature flag creation, including keys, names, filters, and active status.

faq.question: Can it read user profile information?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes tools for current user checks and detailed profile retrieval.

faq.question: Is it safe for production projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. It can change live PostHog state, so use approval steps and least-privilege access.

faq.question: Why does it search tools first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change. Searching first helps the assistant use current parameters and avoid malformed calls.

faq.question: Does it include custom code or scripts?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill is a Markdown workflow guide and does not include executable scripts.
